text: Hey everyone, I'm Sha. In this video, I'll discuss how you can improve your AI apps with error analysis. I'll start by explaining what this is and why we should care about it and then walk through a concrete example where I used this method to get GPT4.1 to write LinkedIn posts in my style. The whole motivation of this method is the fact that large language models are probabilistic systems. In other words, we can't be 100% certain about the output that we're going to get from one of these models, even if you give it the same exact input. For example, if we go to chat GBT or your favorite large language model and you ask it a question like, what does probabilistic mean? You might get a response that looks something like this. Probabilistic means something is based on probability rather than certainty. Instead of always giving the same outcome, it gives different outcomes depending on chance, randomness, or likelihood. But if you open up a new chat window and then paste the same exact input, you might get a different output. That might be probabilistic means something is based on probability. It involves uncertainty and can have multiple possible outcomes each with a certain likelihood. If you do this one more time, you might get probabilistic means based on probability, something that involves chance, uncertainty, or likelihood rather than being fixed or certain. On one hand, the fact that LLMs are probabilistic is actually a really beneficial thing. It allows them to be very flexible and give humanlike responses to a wide range of inputs. But on the other hand, this does present some unique challenges when we're trying to build on top of these models to create software that solves real world problems in a reliable way. So the question here is how do we build reliable software on top of an unpredictable model? And so one answer to this question is error analysis, which consists of identifying a machine learning model's most severe mistakes in order to guide its improvement. In other words, we're trying to figure out what are the most common failures that come up for our machine learning system and resolving those failures in a systematic way. This is kind of like if you have a sink full of dirty dishes. One approach might be just grabbing the dishes at the top of the pile first and washing those. So, grabbing the most convenient ones first. Or an alternative method would be to wash the dishes from the biggest down to the smallest. These two images are showing the resulting pile after just washing three dishes. But you can see if we start with the biggest one, we get the pile down to like 50% of its original size. While this approach up here, the pile looks basically the same. Error analysis is kind of like the second approach. You're still doing work to resolve these errors, but you're getting a much higher return on your effort because you're attacking the problems in an optimal way. That was just a metaphor for error analysis. What does this actually look like in practice? This has been around for a while in machine learning. It's not something specific to large language models. And traditionally, the way this would look is let's say you're trying to do a image classification task. Error analysis would consist of gathering all the mistakes your classification model made, going through them one by one, and doing the following annotations. On the left, we have images that were wrongly classified. And then what we want to do here is to cluster these mistakes into subtypes. I have three subtypes here. Here is a muffin that maybe looks like a Chihuahua. The second subtype is a pet that is in a costume of another animal. Finally, we have an animal cracker. So maybe our model got confused about this. What we're going to do is just go step by step and first identify what error subtype this was. So this is a muffin. In our notes section, we say muffin looks like Chihuahua. So maybe our model got confused and thought this was a Chihuahua. This next error we'll label as a pet in a costume. So we'll say dog is wearing a bear costume. So maybe it's actually a dog, but our model thought it was a bear. And then again, we have another muffin that looks like a chihuahua. And then finally, we have an animal cracker. So the cracker shaped like an elephant. Maybe the model thought this was an elephant when it's actually a cracker. So we'll do this for dozens or even hundreds of examples. And what we might find from this is 50% of our errors come from our model thinking muffins are Chihuahua. This gives us a very powerful insight that our models specifically getting tripped up from this. So maybe we can go analyze our training data to make sure there are no mislabeled examples. Or we might notice that there are no muffin examples in the training data, but there might be a lot of Chihuahua examples. And so that's why the model is getting confused. So maybe the simple solution in that case would be to add a few examples of muffins to help the model. That's an example in traditional machine learning. However, when it comes to large language models, this process isn't so straightforward. And that's because with LLMs, we're typically not trying to do a classification task where there's a clear right or wrong answer, but rather we're getting these models to do open-ended things where there are multiple levels of correctness and wrongness. So to cope with this, we need to refine this error analysis process specifically for large language models. One way we can do this is through the following five-step workflow. We'll start by curating 30 to 100 realistic inputs for our large language model. We'll then pass these inputs to our LLM or larger LLM system to generate outputs for them. Then we'll manually review these outputs one at a time. For this, we'll want to create a custom data annotator. And that's simply because LLM outputs tend to be very long. So if you try to do this in Excel or a similar program, it's going to add a lot of friction to the data annotation process. But I promise you this is much easier than it sounds. Then we will manually review all the input output pairs and write notes on model mistakes. So here unlike in the previous slide, we're not just looking at the errors the model made because we don't know which ones are errors and which ones are not. we have to look at all the outputs and write open-ended notes on them. And then finally, we can review all the open-ended notes and come up with error subtypes or categories and then analyze their frequency. This will basically allow us to triage failures and mistakes that the model is making so we can get the most return on our effort.
